ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chapati entry; phulka is a specific puffed variant cooked directly on flame after initial griddle cooking. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chapatti/roti category; phulka is a puffed sub-variant. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code exists for phulka or chapati as a distinct food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium phulka chapati weighs approximately 30 g and is typically consumed as 2–3 pieces per meal alongside dal, curry, or vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati (without fat) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports chapati without fat (plain whole wheat chapati). Phulka is the puffed variant of plain chapati made from whole wheat atta without added fat, cooked on a tawa then puffed directly on flame. Nutrient profile is directly applicable. Values per 100 g edible portion used.

gi_source.title: Chapati, whole wheat — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports whole wheat chapati GI values in the range of 52–62. A GI of 52 is used here, representing plain whole wheat chapati without added fat, which is the closest analogue to phulka. Phulka is made from the same whole wheat atta dough without fat, puffed on flame; the starch gelatinisation profile is comparable. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 61.0 × 0.30) / 100 = 9.5 g for a 30 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (5 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Phulka chapati is very low in sodium at approximately 5 mg per 100 g, making it a suitable staple for individuals managing hypertension, provided it is paired with low-sodium accompaniments such as plain dal or vegetable curries prepared without added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 52) and significant carbohydrate content (61 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 and a glycemic load of 9.5 per 30 g serving, phulka chapati has a moderate glycaemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 1–2 phulkas per meal and pair with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and whole wheat dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Phulka chapati contains negligible saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and provides 3.9 g dietary fibre per 100 g from whole wheat atta, supporting LDL cholesterol management; it is a heart-friendly staple when consumed without added ghee or butter.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Phulka chapati (also called phulka or fulka) is a thin, soft, unleavened Indian flatbread made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and a pinch of salt. It is a staple in North and South Indian households and is widely consumed across the Indian subcontinent. The dough is rolled into small, thin discs and first cooked on a flat iron griddle (tawa) until partially set, then placed directly on an open flame or hot coil where it puffs up dramatically into a balloon shape due to steam trapped inside — this puffing is the defining characteristic that distinguishes phulka from ordinary chapati. The result is a light, soft, slightly charred flatbread with a delicate wheaty flavour and a tender, layered texture. Phulka is typically served hot, often brushed lightly with ghee or served plain, alongside dal (lentil soup), sabzi (vegetable curry), raita, or pickles. Nutritionally, phulka made without added fat provides approximately 297 kcal per 100 g, with 9 g protein, 1.7 g fat, 61 g carbohydrate, and 3.9 g dietary fibre from whole wheat. Its sodium content is very low (approximately 5 mg per 100 g), making it suitable for hypertension management. The glycemic index of whole wheat chapati is approximately 52 (low GI), and the glycemic load per 30 g serving is approximately 9.5, making it a moderate-impact carbohydrate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled. The whole wheat fibre content supports cardiovascular health by contributing to LDL cholesterol reduction. In Singapore, phulka chapati is served in Indian restaurants and tiffin-style eateries, often as part of a thali or set meal.
common_user_queries: How is phulka different from chapati?
Can I eat phulka if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chapati without fat, which is the closest approved primary source for phulka. Phulka is compositionally identical to plain chapati (same ingredients, no added fat) with only the cooking finish differing (flame puffing). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using whole wheat chapati as the analogous food. No exact phulka-specific entry exists in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN 'Chapati without fat' entry, adapted for phulka which uses identical ingredients and preparation minus added fat. 2. GI of 52 is based on whole wheat chapati from the University of Sydney GI Database; phulka is compositionally equivalent. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plain whole wheat flour and water contain no trans fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — whole wheat flour contains negligible vitamin C, confirmed by ICMR-NIN). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based food with no animal products). 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN chapati entry. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN chapati entry. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN chapati entry. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN chapati entry. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN chapati entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN chapati entry. 5. Serving size of 30 g represents one medium phulka. 6. The cuisine_tag 'Naan' in the input is inaccurate for phulka (naan is leavened and oven-baked); this tag has been excluded from the output. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302397 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as closest parent matches; no exact phulka-specific ontology code exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of the processing date.
